title: Your first request
subtitle: API key, the Messages API, and your first code in Python and TypeScript

sources.title: Working with messages тАФ Claude Docs

theory.md:
So far you've talked to Claude through ready-made interfaces: the claude.ai website, the apps, Claude Code. The **API** (Application Programming Interface) is a way to reach Claude from your own code. Your program sends an HTTP request тАФ Claude returns a response. This is what chatbots, in-product assistants, document-processing pipelines, and agents are built on.

The developer's home is the **Claude platform** (platform.claude.com): that's where the Console, the documentation, and key management live. In the Console you create a developer account, top up your balance, and get your master pass тАФ the API key.

It's important to understand: a claude.ai subscription and the API are **different things with different billing**. A subscription is a fixed price for the interface; the API is pay-as-you-go, charged by the amount of text processed.

theory.text: The API key and keeping it safe

theory.md:
An **API key** is a string like `sk-ant-...`, created in the platform's Console (the API Keys section). The key is your login, your password, and your wallet all at once: anyone who has it can make requests **on your dime**.

Rules for handling the key:

- **Never paste the key directly into your code.** Code goes into git, git goes to GitHub, and keys from public repositories get stolen by bots within minutes.
- Store the key in the **environment variable** `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` тАФ the official SDKs pick it up automatically, and the key never appears in your code at all.
- In projects, use a `.env` file and be sure to add it to `.gitignore`.
- If a key is exposed тАФ immediately **revoke it in the Console** and create a new one. A key that made it into git history is considered compromised forever.

Client-side code (a site in the browser, a mobile app) is also a public place: anything that reaches the user can be extracted. The API key must live **only on the server**. The frontend talks to your server, and your server talks to the Claude API.

theory.text: The Messages API: anatomy of a request

theory.md:
The main endpoint is the **Messages API**: `POST https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages`. Three required parameters:

- **`model`** тАФ which model answers, for example `"claude-opus-4-8"`.
- **`max_tokens`** тАФ the maximum length of the response in tokens (a hard cap: once reached, generation stops).
- **`messages`** тАФ the list of conversation messages. Each message is an object with `role` and `content` fields.

There are two roles in `messages`: **`user`** (your turns) and **`assistant`** (Claude's replies). A conversation always starts with `user`, and the roles alternate. When you send a history of several messages, Claude "sees" the whole conversation and continues it тАФ that's how a multi-turn conversation works, which we'll come back to in the next lesson.

The response comes back as an object: a `content` array of blocks (text is in `content[0].text`), a `stop_reason` (why generation ended), and `usage` тАФ how many tokens went into the input and the output.

theory.title: Python: pip install anthropic
theory.code:
import anthropic

# The key is taken from the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain in two sentences what an API is."}
    ],
)

print(message.content[0].text)
print(message.usage)  # how many tokens were spent

theory.title: TypeScript: npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk

theory.bad: client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-api03-XXXX...")  # key right in the code
theory.good: client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # key lives in the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable
theory.explanation: In the bad version the key is baked into the source: one `git push` and it's in the repository history forever, even if you delete the line later. In the good version the code is clean and the secret lives in the environment: locally in `.env` (added to `.gitignore`), on the server in the hosting settings. The SDK will find the variable itself тАФ the `api_key` argument isn't even needed.

theory.md: The official Quickstart (platform.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart) describes exactly this path: create an API key in the Console, export it as the `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable, install the SDK (`pip install anthropic` or `npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk`), and make your first `client.messages.create` call with the `model`, `max_tokens`, and `messages` parameters.

Don't pick a model at random: every task has its own "weight class." Any current model works fine for first experiments, and we'll cover a deliberate choice by price and quality in the "API economics" lesson. The list of current model identifiers is always in the documentation тАФ they look like `claude-opus-4-8`, `claude-sonnet-4-6`, `claude-haiku-4-5`.

tasks.question: Which roles are valid in the messages array when calling the Messages API?
tasks.explanation: Two roles alternate in `messages`: `user` тАФ your user's turns, and `assistant` тАФ Claude's replies. A conversation always begins with a `user` message. By passing history with both roles, you let Claude see the whole context of the conversation.
tasks.hint: One role is whoever asks, the other is the assistant itself.

tasks.instruction: A developer wrote their first request and is about to commit the code to a public repository. Find the line that will leak money from their account.
tasks.explanation: The API key is hardcoded right in the source. After a push to a public repository, scanner bots will find it тАФ and start making requests on the owner's dime. The right way: remove the `api_key` argument entirely and put the key in the `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable тАФ the `Anthropic()` constructor picks it up automatically. And revoke the exposed key in the Console.

tasks.instruction: Assemble the body of a Messages API request in the right order: model тЖТ response limit тЖТ messages. Drop the parameters that don't exist in the API.
blocks: "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Draft an outline for an article about APIs"}]
tasks.explanation: The minimal valid request is `model`, `max_tokens`, and `messages`. The key is passed not in the request body but in an HTTP header (the SDK does this itself), so the "password" block is a trap. There is no `speed: turbo-max` parameter тАФ don't invent fields: the API rejects requests with unknown parameters.

tasks.question: What happens if Claude's response turns out to be longer than the max_tokens you specified?
options: The API returns a 400 error and you have to retry the request
The response is cut off at the limit, and stop_reason equals "max_tokens"
Claude automatically compresses the response to fit the limit
The limit is ignored тАФ max_tokens is only for statistics
tasks.explanation: `max_tokens` is a hard cap: generation simply stops at it, and the response will have `stop_reason: "max_tokens"` instead of the usual `"end_turn"`. That's why it's useful to check `stop_reason` in your code тАФ a truncated response is easy to mistake for a complete one. The model doesn't know about the limit in advance and doesn't "squeeze" the text to fit it.
tasks.hint: Remember: max_tokens is a constraint that acts during generation, not an instruction for the model.

tasks.instruction:
**Mission: your first real request.** You'll need an API key тАФ it's created in the Console of the Claude platform (platform.claude.com).

1. Go to the platform's Console and create an API key (the **API Keys** section). Copy it right away тАФ the full key is shown only once.
2. Save the key to an environment variable: `export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."` (macOS/Linux) or via the environment settings on Windows.
3. Install the SDK: `pip install anthropic` or `npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk`.
4. Copy the example from the theory, run it, and get a response.
5. Print `message.usage` and see how many tokens your request cost.
checklist: The key is created in the Console and NOT pasted into the code
The ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable is set
The request ran and Claude's response was printed
You looked at usage and know how many tokens were spent
